More Notes: Increased annual energy loss due to snow coverage.
sourcematerial: O. Bamisile et al. Renewable and Sustainable Energy Reviews 208 (2025) 115073 Michigan, US increased annual energy loss from 5 % to 34 % due to snow coverage [199]. Energy losses from snow cover in Munich, Germany amount to 0.3–2.7 % [196], whereas in Russia losses exceed 40 % with 4 mm thick snow coverage [192]. In California, where solar power provides nearly 20 % of electricity, the extreme wildfires in September 2020 reduced solar energy production by 30 % [212]. Similarly, in June 2023, smoke from Canadian wildfires spread to the Northeast and Midwest US, reducing solar generation by up to 60 % in New England [213], and by 25 % in Mid-Atlantic and Midwestern states [214].

More Notes: The solar eclipse on 21 August 2017 caused a reduction in Texas PV output from 7.2 GW to 1.5 GW over 40 minutes, and then a rise back to 5.9 GW in a further 40 minutes.
sourcematerial: In Texas, state-wide solar PV production fell from 7.2 to 1.5 GW in 40 min, and then rose back to 5.9 GW in a further 40 min. A partial solar eclipse occurred in Prague on 20 March 2015 saw 68 % of the solar disc covered at its peak and caused a 69 % reduction in solar PV production [232]. The 2017 eclipse affected almost all of the country’s utility-scale systems. However, this capacity was only 22 GW at the time [234], so this impacted around 2 % of total generating capacity. A subsequent eclipse on 8 April 2024 affected over three times more capacity as shown on the right bar of Fig. 20. Systems in Texas lost more than 90 % of their output during the eclipse.

More Notes: The North American solar eclipse on 21 August 2017 caused a 69% reduction in solar PV production during its peak.
